coredata.dc:title: Structure-modified polymeric carbon-dots with lowered retention and enhanced colloidal stability in porous media for tracer application at extreme reservoir condition
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Wei
Chen, Hsieh
Song, Haofeng
Gizzatov, Ayrat
coredata.dc:description: Tracer technology has been increasingly used in inter-well tests to investigate reservoir performance, reservoir connectivity and residual oil saturation for providing useful information to improve decision making in reservoir management. Stable nanoparticle tracers with high-sensitive real-time detectability are highly desired, and as one of the nanoparticles tracers, carbon dots (C-dots) have been studied and tested in field trial for reservoir monitoring. In this research, we report a modified method to synthesize fluorescent C-dots which fluorinated, sulfonated or zwitterionic functional groups were incorporated into the C-dots. The synthesis reactions occurred at hydrothermal conditions with inexpensive starting materials and are readily to scale up for industrial application. The synthesized C-dots are readily dispersible in brines and exhibit improved colloidal stability in high salinity and at high temperature and lowered retention in reservoir rock. Optical properties of the synthesized colloidal C-dots were studied by UV–visible and fluorescence spectroscopies, and the difference in fluorescence between the C-dots hydrothermally treated at different temperatures enables them to be used as multicolor tracers with fluorescence barcodes. Retentions of the C-dots in porous rocks were evaluated by adsorption in crushed calcite and core flooding tests with limestone, and near zero retention of the modified C-dots in reservoir rock were revealed. In molecular dynamics (MD) simulations, results show the C-dot adhesions on calcite surface follow the decreasing order: plain > sulfonated > zwitterionic > fluorinated C-dots, consistent well with experiments that the functionalized C-dots all have lowered adsorption on limestone. In comparison with those C-dots reported in literature, our results suggest that the synthesized C-dots using the modified procedure have excellent fluorescence properties, improved thermal stability, photostability, and water dispersibility, enabling their use as optically detectable nano-agent tracers for oilfield application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Water tracer
Fluorescence
Retention
Core flooding
Molecular dynamics simulation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Photos of C-dots samples under white or 375-nm UV light. The C-dots at concentration of 0.625 wt% were dispersed in (A) DI water, (B) 35 kppm brine or (C) 125 kppm brine and heated at 95 °C for 7 days.

Two-dimensional fluorescence profiles of C-dots with different dopants synthesized by (A) a hydrothermal process at 190 °C or (B) at 210 °C, or (C) a sequential heating process at 185 °C. Concentration of samples is 0.0625 wt% in DI water.
Normalized tracer concentration in the effluents (C/CInj), mass recovered, and pressure drops vs. injected pore volume in core flooding of C-dots-CAHS.
Normalized tracer concentration profile vs. PVI of pristine and functionalized C-dots.
Fluorescence spectra of 2-FBA modified C-dots in different media: DI water, 35 kppm and 125,000 ppm brines.
Intensity of fluorescence of ANTS modified C-dots at different concentrations in different media. Fluorescence spectra were taken at excitation of 425-nm.
C-dot analogues used in MD simulations.

Representative simulation setup with a 2-FBA functionalized C-dot analogue (G-2FBA) on calcite
(
1
0
1 ‾
4
)
surface. The atom colors are: Ca = green; C = cyan; O = red; H = white; F = pink. The blue and red arrows indicate the approaching and retracting directions in the force measurement simulations. The water and ions are now shown for clarity.

Representative force versus distance measurements from SMD simulations for (A) plain (G-CA) and (B) 2FBA-functionalized C-dots (G-2FBA). The blue curves are with the C-dots approaching to the calcite surfaces (decreasing z), and the red curves are with the C-dots retracting from the calcite surfaces (increasing z). The maximum retracting force are denoted F max.
Averaged F max for the plain (G-CA) and modified C-dots (G-ANTS, G-SPVPB, and G-2FBA) simulated in this work.
Retention of undoped and doped C-dots in limestone in 35 kppm brine, determined by fluorescence spectroscopic method.
Summary of retentions of C-dots in Indiana limestone by core flooding determination.

head.ce:title: Structure-modified polymeric carbon-dots with lowered retention and enhanced colloidal stability in porous media for tracer application at extreme reservoir condition

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Aramco Americas: Aramco Research Center-Boston, Cambridge, MA, 02139, USA
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Aramco Americas: Aramco Research Center-Boston, Cambridge, MA, 02139, USA

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Intern Ph.D. student from University of Texas at Austin.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Tracer technology has been increasingly used in inter-well tests to investigate reservoir performance, reservoir connectivity and residual oil saturation for providing useful information to improve decision making in reservoir management. Stable nanoparticle tracers with high-sensitive real-time detectability are highly desired, and as one of the nanoparticles tracers, carbon dots (C-dots) have been studied and tested in field trial for reservoir monitoring. In this research, we report a modified method to synthesize fluorescent C-dots which fluorinated, sulfonated or zwitterionic functional groups were incorporated into the C-dots. The synthesis reactions occurred at hydrothermal conditions with inexpensive starting materials and are readily to scale up for industrial application. The synthesized C-dots are readily dispersible in brines and exhibit improved colloidal stability in high salinity and at high temperature and lowered retention in reservoir rock. Optical properties of the synthesized colloidal C-dots were studied by UV–visible and fluorescence spectroscopies, and the difference in fluorescence between the C-dots hydrothermally treated at different temperatures enables them to be used as multicolor tracers with fluorescence barcodes. Retentions of the C-dots in porous rocks were evaluated by adsorption in crushed calcite and core flooding tests with limestone, and near zero retention of the modified C-dots in reservoir rock were revealed. In molecular dynamics (MD) simulations, results show the C-dot adhesions on calcite surface follow the decreasing order: plain > sulfonated > zwitterionic > fluorinated C-dots, consistent well with experiments that the functionalized C-dots all have lowered adsorption on limestone. In comparison with those C-dots reported in literature, our results suggest that the synthesized C-dots using the modified procedure have excellent fluorescence properties, improved thermal stability, photostability, and water dispersibility, enabling their use as optically detectable nano-agent tracers for oilfield application.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Crude oil is the main source of hydrocarbons that are used as fuel and petrochemical feedstock in the world. One overriding problem in exploring for hydrocarbons in the subsurface is related to the probing and characterization of an environment that cannot be seen. In oil and gas industry, tracing technology is a very effective tool for obtaining reservoir information [1,2]. For example, cross-well tracers, also referred to as inter-well tracers, can be used to obtain information about reservoir fluid flow patterns by injecting the tracer at an injection location, and subsequently retrieving and analyzing a quantity of the injected tracer at a producing location downstream from the injection location. Tracer test data can be used to validate or update existing reservoir/geological models leading to history matching and production optimization with much greater fidelity.

Carbon dots (C-dots), also referred to as carbon nanoparticles (C-NPs) or carbon quantum dots (CQDs), are a new class of carbon nanomaterials and have attracted growing interest in many fields because of their superiority in water solubility, chemical inertness, low toxicity, ease of functionalization and resistance to photobleaching [3–8]. Using citric acid as precursor for hydrothermal reactions is one of the most commonly used method to produce C-dots, and fluorescence properties of the synthesized polymeric C-dots can be tuned through structural modification by doping in synthesis [9]. Depending on the methods in synthesis, the C-dots can exhibit strong fluorescence in visible spectral range, and the carbon-based fluorescent nano-agents are inexpensive and easy to be detected by fluorescence methods, such fluorescence imaging and spectroscopy. In the oil and gas industry, being an environmentally friendly substance with strong fluorescence and penetration properties, the C-dots as a novel nano-agent tracer have been tested in reservoir [10–17]. It has been demonstrated that the C-dots can adapt to different temperatures, salinity and pH environment in the process of obtaining reservoir information [10,12–14,16]. However, the nanoparticle-based reservoir agents still face challenges in stability and transport through hydrocarbon reservoirs under harsh reservoir environment, typically up to 230,000 ppm of total dissolved solids (TDS) in salinity, 100 °C in temperature and 3200 psi in pressure, without loss or degradation.

In this research, we have developed a modified method to synthesize C-dots with enhanced colloidal stability in harsh reservoir condition and neglectable retention in reservoir rocks by introducing fluorinated, sulfonated and zwitterionic functional groups into the C-dots. The modified C-dots also have different fluorescence features and thus can be identified as multicolored barcode tracers in reservoir application. To understand the C-dot retention mechanism, molecular dynamics (MD) simulations were performed to measure the adhesions of the plain and functionalized C-dot analogues on calcite surfaces, which we demonstrated that the fluorinated, sulfonated and zwitterionic functional groups can all reduce the adhesions thus improve C-dot mobility in reservoir inter-well tracing applications.

Citric acid monohydrate (Certified ACS, Granular) and ethanolamine (Laboratory Grade) were from Fisher. 2, 4-difluoroaniline (2,4-FBA, 98%) and 2,4,6-trifluoroaniline (2,4,6-FBA, 98%) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. 8-Aminonaphthalene-1,3,6-trisulfonic acid, disodium salt (ANTS, 99.0%) and 2,4,6-Trifluorobenzoic acid (≥98.0%) came from TCI. 4-Fluoroaniline (98%) and 4-amino-3-hydroxy-1-naphthalenesulforic acid (AHNS, 90+%, ACS reagent) were products of Acros Organics. 1-(3-sulfopropyl)-2-vinylpyridine betaine (SPVPB) was obtained from Monomer-Polymer and Dajac Labs, l-α-lecithin (Soybean) was from MED Millipore, cocamidopropyl betaine (CAPB, 30% in water) was obtained from Combi-Blocks, and cocamidopropyl hydroxysultaine (CAHS) was obtained from CCI, respectively. Synthetic brines (low salinity at 35,000 ppm and high salinity of 125,000 ppm of TDS) were prepared by dissolving inorganic salts (NaCl, KCl, CaCl2, BaCl2, Na2SO4, and NaHCO3) in deionized (DI) water. DI water with 18.2 MΩ cm was form a Milli-Q water purification system. Crushed powder of limestone was provided by Core Labs.

Carbon dots were synthesized using a modified procedure based on the literature reported method [13]. 4.2 g citric acid was dissolved in 5 mL H2O, and then mixed with 3.66 g ethanolamine. For each sample, 0.5 g organic compounds with different functional groups were added and dissolved in the mixture. Then the samples were hydrothermal treated with three different procedures: (i) heating in autoclaves at 190 °C for 2 h, (ii) heating in autoclaves at 210 °C for 4 h and (iii) heating in open vessels at 80°C-100°C-125°C-150°C-175°C–190 °C sequentially in period of 12 h.

Morphology of the synthesized C-dots have been examined by transmission electron microscope with a JEOL 2010 FEG system under 80 kV and the spherical nanoparticles shown average particle sizes of 2–5 nm (Fig. S1). Fluorescence spectra of the synthesized C-dots were recorded in a Horiba Nanolog-3 spectrometer with a 950 PMT detector at 0.1 s integrate time and 2-nm slit width for both excitation and emission. Colloidal stability was tested in different media (DI water and brines) at 95 °C for 3–7 days. Adsorption of the C-dots on rock were studied by mixing 2 g crushed limestone and 10 mL of 0.1 wt% C-dots suspension in 35 kppm brine in sealed vial and placed in oven at 95 °C for 7 days. The limestone powder was washed by acetone, ethanol and DI water thoroughly and dried at 95 °C before using for the absorption experiment. Colloid concentrations before and after the mixing with rock were compared by both fluorescence and UV–vis spectroscopy to determine the retention of C-dots in the rock.

For retention study of colloidal C-dots in brines, core plugs (batch# 1,901,631) of Indiana limestone were purchased from CoreLab, which have lengths of 3 inches and diameters of 1.5 inches. They were initially saturated with brine before mounting into the core holder. Permeability of the rock was measured before (k) and after (kafter) the experiment at 25 °C. The core plugs used in the study had shown comparable permeabilities and porosities [18], and the core flooding experimental setup is described in Fig. S2. The injecting rate was maintained at 0.2 mL/min, equivalent to the interstitial velocity of 5 ft/day. The injected fluids were preheated in the 95 °C oven through a long-curled tube and had reached the reservoir temperature before entering the rock. The pressure of confining fluids and back pressure regulator was set as 800 psi and 200 psi, respectively. Before the tracer flooding, the core had been flooded with 5 PV of the SW to displace out all dissolved gas. The effluents were collected at the frequency of 0.2 PV/tube.

The representative C-dot analogues used in the MD simulations were prepared as follows. A small graphene substrate was used with a citric acid grafted in the middle that represented the plain C-dot (G-CA). For modified C-dots, zwitterionic SPVPB, sulfonated ANTS, and fluorinated 2-FBA functional groups were grafted on the graphene plate and denoted G-SPVPB, G-ANTS, and G-2FBA, respectively. The C-dot analogues were then placed between extended calcite slabs with low energy
(
1
0
1 ‾
4
)
surfaces in 0.7 M NaCl that represented low salinity brine.

The simulation models and force measurement protocols have been used in our previous studies [19–22]. The calcite surfaces, organic molecules, water, and ions were described by the force fields of Xiao et al. [23], OPLS-AA [24], TIP3P [25], and Åqvist and Chandrasekhar et al. [26] [[,27], respectively, with the geometric combination rule used for the cross terms. MD simulations were performed at temperature T = 300 K and pressure P = 1 bar, using GROMACS v2018.2 package [28]. Periodic boundary conditions were used in three dimensions, with an extended calcite slab facing the C-dot analogues perpendicular to the z axis. The x and y box vectors were both ∼2.5 nm in length and in a 90° angle that complied to the calcite
(
1
0
1 ‾
4
)
surfaces. The space between the calcite slab and its periodic image was set to ∼3.5 nm, where the C-dot analogues were put in the middle. Electrostatic interactions were calculated using the particle-mesh Ewald summation, with a real-space cutoff of 1 nm, a grid spacing of 0.16 nm, and fourth-order interpolation. The van der Waals and neighbor-list cutoffs were both set to 1 nm. We used velocity rescaling temperature coupling with a time constant of 0.5 ps and Berendsen semi-isotropic pressure coupling with a time constant of 5 ps. The simulation time step was set to 2 fs.

Before the production runs, the systems were pre-equilibrated with a steepest-descent energy minimization. The production runs consisted of three consecutive steps: (1) 10 ns equilibration run with restrained C-dot substrate, (2) 20 ns non-equilibrium steered molecular dynamics (SMD) [23,29–31] run with the C-dot substrate approached to the calcite surfaces with constant velocity (0.05 nm/ns), and (3) 20 ns SMD run with the C-dot substrate retracted from the calcite surfaces with constant velocity (0.05 nm/ns). The bottom two layers of the calcite molecules were restrained in order to support the surface, while the top two layers of the calcite molecules could freely move. All bonds with H-atoms were constrained using the LINCS algorithm [32] with the exception of water molecules that were constrained using the SETTLE algorithm [33].

We have synthesized C-dots with different functional groups by a modified procedure based on the method developed by Giannelis et al. [13] C-dots are formed by thermally induced condensation and carbonization of a mixture of citric acid and ethanolamine. The reaction begins with simple neutralization of the acid with amine to form a well-defined complex and organic compound was added to introduce functional groups, and then followed by a hydrothermal treatment at 185–210 °C. Three types of organic compounds with fluorinated, sulphonated or zwitterionic functional groups were chosen to dope the C-dots through co-carbonization during the formation of C-dots, because it has been demonstrated that those functional groups could highly enhance colloidal stability at harsh environment and lower retention in porous media in literature reports [34,35]. The structures of the organic compounds applied in our synthesis are displayed as follows:

Type I: Fluorinated compounds.
Image 1

Type II: Sulfonated compounds.
Image 2

Type III: Zwitterionic compounds.
Image 3

When the synthesis was performed using the literature reported procedure through evaporation of water by heating in an open vessel and prolonged heating of the residual molten salt at 200–230 °C [12], we notice that the resulting C-dots only emit weak fluorescence. By comparing the samples synthesized at different reaction temperature, the strongest fluorescence emission was observed for the C-dots formed at 185 °C by the prolonged heating. At temperature higher than 185 °C, the residual molten salt might be over carbonized and thus lose some fluorophores in C-dots structure. Therefore, in the following syntheses, we performed the reactions by prolonged heating at 185 °C. The prolonged heating was controlled sequentially by 100°C-6 hrs, 125°C-2 hrs, 150°C-2 hrs, 175°C-1hr and then 185°C-1hr. We also performed the synthesis by hydrothermal process in an autoclave and observed strongest fluorescence emission for the formed C-dots at 190 °C.

All the C-dots doped with different organic compounds can well-disperse in water and brines (35,000 ppm and 125,000 ppm of TDS). In stability test of colloids, after the C-dots samples at concentration 0.625 wt% were placed at 95 °C and kept for 7 days, no precipitation was observed for the C-dots undoped and doped by CAHS, ANTS, 2-FBA, SPVPB and AHNS, while slight aggregation was observed for the C-dots doped by lecithin, CAPB and 2,4,6-FBA, as shown by photos in Fig. 1 and also confirmed by dynamic light scattering measurements. Under UV light illumination, all the C-dots samples exhibit fluorescence, implying that the organic dopants do not quench the emission of C-dots.

Fluorescence properties of the C-dots modified with different functional groups and produced with different procedures were systematically studied by fluorescence spectroscopy, and 2D fluorescent profiles of several typical samples are shown in Fig. 2 . In comparison with the undoped C-dots, the doped C-dots do not exhibit significant changes in their fluorescence, although slight modification in fluorescent profiles are observed implying the chemical incorporation of the organic dopants in the C-dots.

The C-dots produced with different heating processes exhibit different fluorescence properties. Comparing the samples hydrothermally treated at 190 °C and 210 °C, with the increase of temperature the excitation and emission maxima of fluorescence shifts to red wavelength by ∼50 nm and ∼70 nm, respectively, although the emission intensity slightly decreased for the samples treat at 210 °C. The fluorescence features of the C-dots treated at 210 °C by hydrothermal process are similar to those of C-dots treated by prolonged heating at 185 °C, however, the fluorescence is weaker for the latter. The difference in fluorescence between the C-dots hydrothermally treated at different temperature enable them to use as multicolored barcode tracers.

Retentions of the C-dots in reservoir rocks were evaluated by mixing the C-dots with crushed powder of limestone. Results in Table 1 show that the C-dots modified by 2-FBA, 2,4-FBA with fluoro groups, by ANTS, APTS and AHNS with sulfonic groups, and by CAHS, CAPB and SPVPB with zwitterionic groups have neglectable adsorption on rocks, ∼100% remaining in suspensions. The C-dots doped by 2,4,6-FBA with multiple fluoro groups and by lecithin with phosphonate group have slightly increased retention in limestone, in comparison with the undoped C-dots.

Retention for selected C-dots in reservoir rocks were further quantified by core flooding experiments with limestone cores. The effluents were transferred to quartz cuvette and scanned by the UV–Vis spectrophotometer; according to the light absorbance, the tracer concentration was extrapolated based on the linear relationship illustrated in Fig. S3. The retention was calculated from the mass difference of the injected and collected tracer using Equation 1,2 and 3 (supporting information).

Fig. 3 demonstrates the developing trend of collected tracer concentration (CCol) over the injected tracer concentration (CInj) with the injected pore volume in core flooding test using the C-dots functionalized with zwitterionic groups. The tracer was noticed in the effluents after 1.4 PVI, and its concentration steadily increased to the injected value after 4 PVI. The breakthrough delay of the tracer was due to the volume of effluent tubes which is around 1 PV. The tracer injection had been continued for 5.5 PV, after that the brine injection was initiated at the same rate (∼5 ft/day). The decline of tracer concentration in the effluents was noticed after flooding 1.3 PV of the brine, which gradually decreased to 0 after another 3.7 PV of injection. All tracers had been flooded during the brine injection, indicating no irreversible adsorption of the C-dots in the tested rock. No plugging issue was noticed, as verified by the steady pressure drops through the experiment.

Effects of media on fluorescence of the C-dots were also studied in water and brines at different salinity, and results show that the fluorescence was enhanced in 35 kppm brine while was quenched slightly in 125 kppm brine, as showed by spectra of an example sample, 2-FBA doped C-dots in Fig. 5 . Detectability of the synthesized C-dots was studied in different media and the limit of detection (LOD) was determined by fluorescence spectroscopic method. In diluted suspensions of samples, the fluorescence of C-dots is quantitatively detectable until less than 10 ppb in water and brines, indicating very low LOD, as demonstrated by example sample of ANTS doped C-dots in Fig. 6 . The high colloidal stability and low retention in rock at reservoir condition, optical barcoding capability and high detectability by fluorescence spectroscopy enable these modified C-dots to be excellent candidates of nano-agent tracer for reservoir application.

We performed MD simulations to have further understanding of the retention mechanism for the plain and modified C-dots on reservoir rock surfaces. Fig. 7 shows the representative C-dot analogues for the MD simulations, where we assumed that the citric acids are the dominant functional groups on the plain C-dot surfaces. On the other hand, for the modified C-dots, we put the functional groups on the surface through the most probable bonding. Fig. 8 shows a snapshot for our MD simulation setup, which we used the atomic force microscopy (AFM) type force measurements in simulations that have been able to reproduce experimental results in our previous studies [20,21]. Fig. 9 shows the representative force measurements from the SMD simulations with the plain C-dot (G-CA) and FBA-modified C-dot (G-2FBA) first approaching (blue curves; decreasing z) and then retracting from the calcite surfaces (red curves; increasing z). As shown, the maximum retraction force (F max) was higher for G-CA than G-2FBA, indicating higher retention for the plain C-dots, which was consistent with experimental column experiments. Fig. 10 shows the averaged F max for the plain and modified C-dots studied in the MD simulations. As shown, the adhesion forces followed the decreasing order: G-CA > G-ANTS > G-SPVPB > G-2FBA, where the modified C-dots all have smaller adhesion forces than the plain C-dots, and the 2FBA functionalized C-dots have the lowest adhesion due to the nonstick nature of the fluorinated groups.

A modified method has been presented to synthesize carbon dots by hydrothermal process, and different functional groups were introduced into the C-dots through co-condensation and co-carbonization of citric acid, ethanolamine and organic compounds with fluorinated, sulfonated and zwitterionic groups. The functionalized C-dots have enhanced colloidal stability in simulated reservoir condition at high salinity and high temperature, and also lowered retention on reservoir rocks as confirmed by adsoprtion and core flooding experiments with limestone. The C-dots treated at different hydrothermal conditions exhibit different fluorescence feature which enables them to use as multicolored barcode tracers. MD simulations demonstrated that the functionalized C-dots all have lowered adhesions on calcite surfaces than plain C-dots, which are consistent with the adsorption experiments where we observed limited retentions for the plain C-dots but 100% recovery for most of the functionalized C-dots.

W. W. performed synthesis and spectroscopic analyses. H. C. performed simulation studies. H. S. and A. G. conducted core flooding tests for retention quantification. W. W. and H. C. designed research and wrote the manuscript, and all authors reviewed the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
